risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Those eligible were listed in alphabetical order by surname. They were then selected randomly from the patient list, using a computer-generated random numbers table." and "They were then randomly assigned to one of the two groups in this study"; also "the participants were then asked by the principal researcher to draw a sealed opaque envelope, in which a number card indicated to which group they had been allocated." Comment: The study explicitly describes a computer-generated random numbers table and random assignment, which is a proper random method for sequence generation.

Quote: "Written consent was obtained in a face-to-face interview and the participants were then asked by the principal researcher to draw a sealed opaque envelope, in which a number card indicated to which group they had been allocated (1 = psycho-education group and 2 = routine care group)." Comment: The study used envelopes described as sealed and opaque, but it does not state that they were sequentially numbered. Under Cochrane criteria, envelope methods are low risk only if all SNOSE features are explicitly reported (sequentially numbered, opaque, sealed). Because the report describes an envelope method lacking the full safeguards, allocation concealment is judged high risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants (n = 34) received a 36-week program of mutual support and the conventional psychiatric outpatient care..." and "Participants (n = 34) received the conventional psychiatric outpatient and family services." Also, "Except for the principal researcher and the group instructor, all other clinic staffs were blinded to treatment allocation." Comment: The intervention and control conditions were clearly different, so participants and the personnel delivering the intervention (principal researcher/group instructor) necessarily knew allocation; the paper explicitly indicates only other clinic staff were blinded. Because the main outcomes were largely subjective/questionnaire-based (FBIS burden, FAD family functioning, FSSI service needs/use, and caregiver-completed measures), knowledge of assignment could plausibly influence outcomes.

Quote: "An independent assessor (a research assistant) was trained to undertake measurements at Time 1, 2 and 3 using a set of questionnaires." and "Except for the principal researcher and the group instructor, all other clinic staffs were blinded to treatment allocation." and "Subjects completed a battery of the Chinese versions of four scales: Family Burden Interview Schedule (FBIS), Family Assessment Device (FAD), Family Support Services Index (FSSI), and Specific Level of Functioning Scale (SLOF)." Comment: The outcome assessor appears to be an independent research assistant, but the report does not explicitly state that this assessor was blinded to allocation. More importantly, several outcomes were subjective/judgement-dependent questionnaires completed by caregivers and involving interview/rating components (FBIS, FAD, FSSI, and SLOF), and participants in this psychoeducation versus routine care trial were necessarily aware of their group assignment (e.g. attending 18 psychoeducation sessions versus routine care). For these self-reported caregiver outcomes, the patient/family participant is effectively the assessor, so lack of participant blinding creates a high risk of detection bias. Although hospital admission data were obtained from records and are objective, the worst-outcome rule applies because unblinded subjective outcomes were also measured.

Quote: "Thirty-one subjects (91.2%) from the mutual psychoeducation and support group completed the program. These subjects, together with those who dropped out or absented in more than four group sessions - psycho-education group (n = 3) and routine care group (n = 2), were evaluated at three times of outcome measurements." and "Analysis of data was on an intention-to-treat basis" and "All subjects, who completed the intervention or not, were followed up over a 12-month post-intervention period." Comment: Randomized n=34 per arm. Post-randomization non-completion of the intervention was 3/34 vs 2/34, but the paper explicitly states these participants were still evaluated at all three outcome time points and analysed by ITT. Thus the analysed outcome appears to include all randomized participants at the 12-month follow-up, with essentially complete outcome data and minimal imbalance; reported reasons for dropout/discontinuation do not suggest important differential missing outcome data.
